channel.title: R on Tyler Burleigh
channel.description: Recent content in R on Tyler Burleigh

item.title: Comparing coronavirus trends in US, Italy, and China
item.description: A simple prediction of coronavirus spread in the US, using case trends in China and Italy.

item.title: Is it safe to go outside?: Coronavirus on-peak/off-peak place indicator
item.description: One of the best behavioral strategies for mitigating the risk of coronavirus infection is “social distancing”. On the personal level, this means keeping a distance between yourself and others, ideally by staying at home. But for most individuals staying at home isn’t possible 24⁄7, even if you have the luxury of working from home, sometimes you gotta go outside to do stuff. There are times when you must go outside, such as going to the grocery store to buy fresh produce.

item.title: Categorical features: cardinality and sparsity
item.description: Some observations about high-cardinality features and sparsity.

item.title: A simple Twilio SMS frontend using Nodejs
item.description: We’re starting to use SMS at work for communicating with customers, and there was a need for a tool that would allow us to send SMS messages and check the history of calls and messages to the phone number we are sending from. With that in mind, I coded a simple frontend to the Twilio API. The frontend is a page with 4 navigation tabs that allow sending a single SMS, sending SMS in bulk, seeing the status of sent SMS messages, and fetching the inbound call/SMS log.

item.title: Predicting customer churn
item.description: Continuing to practice my python skills. I decided to try modeling the Telco Customer Churn dataset from Kaggle. Churn is when customers end their relationship with a company (e.g., by cancelling their subscription to a service). Companies want to retain customers, so understanding and preventing churn is naturally an important goal.

item.title: Predicting survival on the Titanic (with Python!)
item.description: I wanted to try out some of what I’ve learned with python for data science. I thought: Why not try it on the Kaggle Titanic challenge?

item.title: 2019 Data Science Bowl
item.description: I was inspired to participate in Kaggle’s 2019 Data Science Bowl. In this post I link to some of my exploratory analysis and predictive models.

item.title: Simple Moving Average algorithm & backtest
item.description: I made an algorithmic trader in R based on the “moving average crossover” technical indicator and performed a backtest using the TSLA stock. It did not perform well, but it was an interesting challenge that involved some timeseries analysis.

item.title: Predicting t-shirt size from height and weight
item.description: Using body measurement data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES), I created a model that predicts Gildan t-shirt sizes from height and weight.

item.title: Scraping the Mural Arts Philadephia website
item.description: A mini-tutorial on web scraping with R.

item.title: nutritionR: NLP nutrition analysis in R
item.description: I wrote an R package for the Nutritionix API to do nutrition analysis on foods and recipes.

item.title: Yet Another Data Science Job Market Analysis
item.description: An analysis of the data science job market from scraped LinkedIn data

item.title: pyMTurkR (vignette): A HIT with a Qualification Test
item.description: A tutorial on using pyMTurkR to create a HIT with a Qualification Test.

item.title: pyMTurkR: An R package for MTurk Requesters
item.description: I wrote an R package for the MTurk Requester API because MTurkR was being deprecated. I call it “pyMTurkR” because it uses R reticulate to interact with the python boto3 library for API access.

item.title: What is fair payment on MTurk?
item.description: A mini research study on what MTUrk workers consider to be fair compensation. I have used MTurk a lot in the past, so I wanted to know if what I was paying was fair.

item.title: Surviving the Titanic with R caret
item.description: I use R to tackle the Kaggle Titanic data science competition.

item.title: About Me
item.description: For more than ten years, I’ve been working at the intersection of behavior change and data science. From academia to healthcare tech startups to being a part of the industry’s first Behavioral Science team, I’ve been applying rigorous scientific methods and data analytics to improve decision-making in business, always with the goal of driving towards decisions that make the world a better place.

item.title: Publications
item.description: I’ve written things about science and some were peer-reviewed and published. As of 2020-03-17, my papers were cited 367 times and my h-index was 8. (source: Google Scholar) Peer-Reviewed Publications Uncanny Valley Burleigh, T. J. (2015). A challenge to the study of individual differences in uncanny valley sensitivity: The importance of looking at individual-level response patterns. Interaction Studies, 16(2). [PDF] Ferrey, A., Burleigh, T. J., & Fenske, M.

item.title: Teaching
item.description: I’ve given educational talks and lectures from time to time. Here are some of the talks I’ve given in industry settings for non-academic audiences. Research 2020/02 - Control in research design I talk about what “control” means in research design, and what some basic controlled research designs look like. Psychology 2019/04 - A whirlwind tour of persuasion psychology I talk about the foundations of persuasion psychology. Covers human evolution, irrationality, heuristics and biases, cognitive dissonance, and social norms.
